questions.questionText: Which of the following is characteristic of Centriacinar emphysema?
questions.optionA: Strong association with smoking and involvement of the upper lobes
questions.optionB: Involvement of the lower lobes predominantly
questions.explanation: Smoke accumulates in the upper lobes, destroying the respiratory bronchioles.

questions.questionText: A 65-year-old male with a long smoking history presents with a chronic productive cough for 3 months in 2 consecutive years. The most likely diagnosis is:
questions.explanation: The clinical definition of chronic bronchitis requires this specific history.

questions.questionText: Curschmann spirals and Charcot-Leyden crystals in sputum are pathognomonic for:
questions.explanation: Curschmann spirals are mucus plugs; Charcot-Leyden crystals are from breakdown of eosinophils.

questions.questionText: Permanent abnormal dilation of bronchi and bronchioles caused by destruction of muscle and elastic tissue is termed:
questions.explanation: Usually secondary to severe or chronic necrotizing infections.

questions.questionText: Pneumoconiosis caused by inhalation of silica dust typically results in:
questions.optionB: Nodular fibrosis with 'eggshell' calcification in hilar lymph nodes
questions.explanation: Silicosis profoundly increases susceptibility to tuberculosis.

questions.questionText: Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and non-caseating granulomas are the hallmark of:
questions.explanation: Sarcoidosis is a systemic disease of unknown etiology characterized by these findings.

questions.questionText: The most common type of primary lung cancer is:
questions.explanation: Adenocarcinoma is the most common lung cancer overall, particularly in women and non-smokers.

questions.questionText: Which lung cancer is centrally located, strongly associated with smoking, and may produce parathyroid hormone-related peptide (PTHrP)?
questions.explanation: Squamous cell carcinoma leads to hypercalcemia through PTHrP production.

questions.questionText: Small cell carcinoma of the lung typically presents with:
questions.optionB: A large peripheral mass with cavitation
questions.optionC: A favorable response to surgical resection
questions.optionD: Early early metastasis and paraneoplastic syndromes (e.g., ADH, ACTH)
questions.explanation: It is an aggressive neuroendocrine tumor rarely treated with surgery.

questions.questionText: The classic four stages of lobar pneumonia (pneumococcal) are sequentially:
questions.optionC: Congestion, Red Hepatization, Gray Hepatization, Resolution
questions.explanation: This defines the classic lobar pattern of Streptococcus pneumoniae.

questions.questionText: In Respiratory Pathology, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Pathology texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Pathology references for Respiratory Pathology. Subject: Pathology. Chapter: Respiratory Pathology.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Respiratory Pathology scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Pathology. Chapter: Respiratory Pathology.
